schools.summary: The caution: 'my brethren, be not many masters (didaskaloi — teachers; not many of you should become teachers), knowing that we shall receive the greater condemnation (krima — judgment)' — James warning against too many eagerly taking up the role of teacher in the church (presuming to instruct others, the ambition to teach, to assume authority and display knowledge, without the gravity the office requires); those who teach judged more strictly, held to a higher standard, answerable for their words and example, the greater responsibility bringing greater accountability (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: My brethren, be not many masters, knowing that we shall receive the greater condemnation
the warning against many becoming teachers
the ambition to teach checked
teachers judged more strictly

schools.summary: The humbling admission and the test: 'for in many things we offend (ptaiomen — stumble, fail) all (all of us). If any man offend not in word (does not stumble in speech), the same is a perfect (teleios — mature, complete) man, and able also to bridle (chalinagogesai — rein in, control) the whole body' — everyone, teachers included, stumbling in many ways (universal human frailty, reason for caution in teaching); the one who never errs in speech a fully mature man (control of the tongue the surest mark of completeness, for the tongue is the hardest member to govern); and if he can control the tongue (the most unruly member), he can control his whole self, mastery of the tongue implying mastery of all (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: in many things we offend all
If any man offend not in word, the same is a perfect man, and able also to bridle the whole body
the one who controls his tongue is a perfect man
mastery of the tongue implying mastery of all

schools.summary: The first illustration (the tongue compared to a bit): 'behold, we put bits (the bridle-bit) in the horses' mouths, that they may obey us; and we turn about (guide, steer) their whole body' — a small bit in the horse's mouth controlling and directing the whole animal, a tiny instrument governing a great body; the bit making the horse obey, the rider governing the powerful animal by this small means; the point being the disproportion between the small instrument and the great effect — as the small bit turns the whole horse, so the tongue, though small, governs and directs the whole man (the figure preparing the argument that a little member has great power, v.5) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: we put bits in the horses' mouths, that they may obey us; and we turn about their whole body
the figure of the bit that turns the horse
a small instrument governing a great body
the tongue governing the whole man

schools.summary: The second illustration: 'behold also the ships, which though they be so great, and are driven of fierce winds, yet are they turned about (steered) with a very small helm (pedaliou — rudder), whithersoever the governor (the pilot, steersman) listeth (wills, desires)' — a huge ship, driven by strong winds, steered by a tiny rudder according to the pilot's will; the contrast striking (the ship huge and driven by powerful winds, yet a small rudder controls it, the greater the disproportion the more forceful the point); as the small rudder steers the great ship, so the small tongue directs the whole course of a man's life, the two figures (bit and helm) together establishing that a little member wields great power (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the ships, though they be so great... are turned about with a very small helm
the figure of the small helm steering the great ship
a small rudder governing a great ship
the tongue directing the whole course of life

schools.summary: The application of the two figures: 'even so the tongue is a little member (a small part of the body), and boasteth great things (megalauchei — makes great claims, achieves or vaunts great effects). Behold, how great a matter (hylen — forest, or pile of wood) a little fire kindleth (sets ablaze)!' — like the bit and the rudder, the tongue small but accomplishing (or boasting of) great things for good or ill, its power and boasts out of all proportion to its size; the new figure of fire (a tiny spark setting a great forest ablaze) shifting from control to destruction, introducing the tongue's power to do immense harm (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: the tongue is a little member, and boasteth great things
how great a matter a little fire kindleth
the tongue a little member boasting great things, like a small fire
the small tongue with vast power

schools.summary: The dread description, figures piled up: 'and the tongue is a fire (a destructive blaze), a world of iniquity (a whole world of wickedness, the sum and microcosm of evil): so is the tongue among our members, that it defileth (pollutes, stains) the whole body, and setteth on fire the course of nature (ton trochon tes geneseos — the wheel of nature/birth, the whole round or circle of life, a difficult phrase meaning the entire course of human existence); and it is set on fire of hell (gehennes — its evil fire kindled from hell itself)' — the tongue like a destructive fire containing a world of evil, capable of every kind of sin, corrupting the whole person and setting ablaze the entire course of life, its destructive fire ultimately kindled by hell (the source of its corrupting power, a diabolical origin) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: the tongue is a fire, a world of iniquity
it defileth the whole body, and setteth on fire the course of nature; and it is set on fire of hell
the tongue as a fire and a world of iniquity
its destructive fire kindled from hell

schools.summary: The sweep of the statement: 'for every kind (phusis — nature, species) of beasts, and of birds, and of serpents, and of things in the sea, is tamed, and hath been tamed (subdued, domesticated) of (by) mankind' — every class of animal (land beasts, birds, reptiles, sea creatures) having been and being tamed by human skill; man, given dominion over the creatures, succeeding in taming even the wildest of every kind (human ingenuity subduing the animal world); this universal taming setting up the startling contrast of v.8 — man tames every beast, yet cannot tame his own tongue, the most masterful creature unable to master his own small member (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: every kind of beasts, and of birds, and of serpents, and of things in the sea, is tamed... of mankind
every kind of creature has been tamed by man
human mastery over the animal world
the foil for the untamable tongue

schools.summary: The startling climax: 'but the tongue can no man tame; it is an unruly (akatastaton — restless, unstable, ungovernable) evil, full of deadly poison' — in contrast to all the tamable creatures (v.7), the tongue defying human taming (no one able to fully subdue it by his own power, the one untamable thing); a restless, uncontrollable source of mischief, never at rest, always liable to break out in evil; and 'full of deadly poison' like a serpent's venom (slander, lies, and cursing being deadly venom, words that can kill, the figure of the most dangerous creature applied to the tongue) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: But the tongue can no man tame; it is an unruly evil, full of deadly poison
no man can tame the tongue
a restless, ungovernable evil
full of deadly serpent's venom

schools.summary: The tongue's glaring contradiction: 'therewith bless we God, even the Father; and therewith curse we men, which are made after the similitude (likeness, image) of God' — the same tongue used to bless, praise, and worship God the Father (its highest and proper use) and to curse, revile, and wish evil to fellow men (the very same instrument used for blessing God used for cursing men); the inconsistency worse because the men cursed bear God's likeness, so that to curse them is to dishonour the image of God in them, the cursing reflecting on their Maker (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: therewith bless we God, even the Father; and therewith curse we men
which are made after the similitude of God
the inconsistency of blessing God and cursing men with the same tongue
cursing God's image in men

schools.summary: The summary of the inconsistency and the rebuke: 'out of the same mouth proceedeth blessing and cursing. My brethren, these things ought not so to be' — from one and the same mouth coming both blessing (of God) and cursing (of men), the two utterly opposed streams flowing from a single source; the wrongness of one mouth producing such contradictory things (the mouth that praises God should not also curse, the contradiction unnatural and indefensible); a plain, earnest rebuke that such inconsistency is wrong and must not continue, the believer's speech to be consistent, all of one kind, befitting one who blesses God (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Out of the same mouth proceedeth blessing and cursing
My brethren, these things ought not so to be
blessing and cursing should not come from one mouth
the unnatural contradiction rebuked

schools.summary: The rhetorical question from nature: 'doth a fountain send forth at the same place (opening, hole) sweet (fresh) water and bitter (brackish, salt)?' — a single spring not pouring out both fresh and bitter water from the same opening (nature not producing such contradiction); a fountain yielding one kind of water, not two opposite kinds, sweet and bitter not flowing from one source; the figure exposing the unnaturalness of the tongue's double output — as a fountain cannot give both sweet and bitter, so the mouth ought not to give both blessing and cursing (v.10), the consistency of nature rebuking the inconsistency of the tongue (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Doth a fountain send forth at the same place sweet water and bitter
the figure of the fountain that cannot yield both sweet and bitter
nature yielding one kind, not contradictory streams
nature's rebuke of the inconsistent tongue

schools.summary: The further illustrations from nature (the law of kind): 'can the fig tree, my brethren, bear olive berries? either a vine, figs? so can no fountain both yield salt water and fresh' — each tree bearing its own proper fruit (a fig tree cannot bear olives, nor a vine figs, nature holding each plant to its own kind), and just so no spring yielding both salt and fresh water (the source determining the output, consistently after its kind); as nature is consistent (each source yielding only its own kind), so the heart and tongue should be consistent — a heart truly devoted to God will not pour out cursing, the inconsistency of blessing and cursing betraying a divided or unrenewed heart (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: Can the fig tree bear olive berries? either a vine, figs? so can no fountain both yield salt water and fresh
the figures of tree and vine: each yields after its kind
the source determining the output
the heart and tongue should be consistent

schools.summary: The question opening the theme of wisdom: 'who is a wise man (sophos) and endued with knowledge (epistemon — understanding, skilled) among you? let him shew (demonstrate) out of a good conversation (kales anastrophes — good conduct, manner of life) his works with meekness of wisdom' — James asking who claims to be truly wise and understanding, the question testing the nature of real wisdom; the answer being that true wisdom is proved not by words or claims but by a good life and good deeds (as faith is shown by works, ch. 2, so wisdom is shown by conduct), done 'with meekness of wisdom' (the gentleness that belongs to true wisdom) — genuine wisdom humble and gentle, not proud or contentious, meekness its hallmark, distinguishing it from the bitter, self-assertive false wisdom (vv.14-16) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Who is a wise man and endued with knowledge among you? let him shew out of a good conversation his works with meekness of wisdom
true wisdom is shown by a good life with meekness
wisdom proved by conduct, not words
meekness the hallmark of true wisdom

schools.summary: The contrast with meek wisdom: 'but if ye have bitter envying (zelon pikron — bitter zeal, jealousy) and strife (eritheian — selfish ambition, rivalry, factiousness) in your hearts, glory not (do not boast), and lie not against the truth' — the marks of false wisdom being bitter jealousy and self-seeking contention harboured in the heart; those with such bitter, contentious hearts not to boast of being wise (their inward envy and strife belying any claim to wisdom, the boast empty); to claim wisdom while full of bitter envy being to 'lie against the truth' (to contradict reality, such a claim a lie, for true wisdom and bitter strife cannot coexist) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: if ye have bitter envying and strife in your hearts, glory not, and lie not against the truth
the warning against bitter envy and strife masquerading as wisdom
jealousy and contention the marks of false wisdom
claiming wisdom amid envy a lie

schools.summary: The verdict on the false wisdom: 'this wisdom descendeth not from above (is not from God, not heavenly), but is earthly (epigeios — belonging to the earth, worldly, concerned only with the things of this world), sensual (psychike — natural, animal, unspiritual, springing from the unrenewed natural man, the animal soul, not the Spirit), devilish (daimoniodes — demon-like, from demons, sharing their envy and strife)' — the wisdom marked by bitter envy and strife not coming from God but of a threefold base origin, a wisdom of the flesh and the world, descending from below and partaking of the very nature of evil spirits (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: This wisdom descendeth not from above, but is earthly, sensual, devilish
the threefold character of false wisdom
worldly, natural, and demon-like
not from God but from below

schools.summary: The consequence: 'for where envying (jealousy) and strife (selfish ambition, faction) is, there is confusion (akatastasia — disorder, instability, tumult) and every evil work (every kind of base deed)' — wherever bitter jealousy and contention reign, the result being disorder and all manner of evil; 'confusion' the social disorder, instability, and tumult that envy and strife produce (division, unrest, and chaos in the community, the opposite of the peace that marks heavenly wisdom); 'every evil work' — envy and strife opening the door to all kinds of wickedness, once they reign no evil excluded, the fruit of false wisdom comprehensive corruption (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: where envying and strife is, there is confusion and every evil work
envy and strife breed confusion and every evil
disorder the fruit of false wisdom
comprehensive corruption, the opposite of peace

schools.summary: The catalogue of heavenly wisdom's qualities: 'but the wisdom that is from above (from God, heavenly) is first pure (hagne — chaste, holy, free from defilement, the foremost and fundamental quality, placed first deliberately), then peaceable (peace-loving, not contentious), gentle (epieikes — forbearing, considerate, reasonable), and easy to be intreated (eupeithes — willing to yield, compliant, open to persuasion), full of mercy and good fruits (compassionate and fruitful in good deeds), without partiality (adiakritos — impartial, undivided, not wavering), and without hypocrisy (anypokritos — sincere, genuine)' — true wisdom first of all pure, then peace-making, gentle and forbearing, reasonable and ready to yield, merciful and active in good, impartial and sincere; the complete portrait of the wisdom from God, every quality the opposite of the bitter, contentious false wisdom (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the wisdom that is from above is first pure, then peaceable, gentle, and easy to be intreated
full of mercy and good fruits, without partiality, and without hypocrisy
the sevenfold description of the wisdom from above
every quality the opposite of false wisdom

schools.summary: The harvest of heavenly wisdom: 'and the fruit of righteousness (the fruit which is righteousness, or which righteousness yields) is sown in peace of (by) them that make peace (the peacemakers)' — the harvest of righteousness coming from seed sown in peace by those who are peaceable, peace being the soil and the manner in which righteousness grows; righteousness produced not amid strife but in peace (the peaceable disposition, the mark of heavenly wisdom, v.17, being the condition under which the fruit of righteousness is sown and grows — strife yields confusion, v.16, but peace yields righteousness); the peacemakers the sowers whose harvest is righteousness, the chapter closing by commending the peaceable spirit as the soil of all good fruit (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace of them that make peace
the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by peacemakers
righteousness growing in the soil of peace
the peaceable spirit the soil of all good fruit
